TCS TCS has three stages for campus hiring: TCS NQT (National Qualifier Test) with aptitude, verbal, and coding; followed by Technical Interview and HR Interview for those who clear NQT. TCS tests fundamentals — basic data structures, OOP concepts, database basics, and technical knowledge of your projects. They don't test hard LeetCode problems in technical interviews. NQT has multiple sections: numerical ability, verbal, logical reasoning, and a coding section with 1-2 easy-medium problems. The coding section matters most for differentiation. Not being able to explain your own projects, weak basics in C/Java/Python, or not knowing fundamental CS concepts like OOPS, DBMS, OS. For TCS, aptitude preparation matters as much as coding. Practice GeeksforGeeks easy-medium problems. Know your projects inside out — 'tell me about your project' is guaranteed. TCS pays 3.36 LPA base, 7 LPA for NQT Digital roles — target the Digital variant.

Infosys InfyTQ certification + campus placement OR direct InStep internship route. Technical interview has basic coding, CS fundamentals, and HR round. Infosys also has Specialist Programmer track (higher package) with harder coding. SP track (Specialist Programmer, ~6.5 LPA) requires stronger DSA. Regular track (3.6 LPA) tests only basics. Target SP track if you have decent DSA — it's worth the extra prep. Hackwithinfy is the online test for SP track — medium-hard problems. Regular placement has easier aptitude + basic coding. For SP track, not being able to solve medium problems under time pressure. For regular track, being unable to explain CS fundamentals. If your CGPA is above 6.5, you qualify for campus placement. For SP track, HackWithInfy prep needs LeetCode medium problems. Register for InfyTQ early — the certification helps in regular placement track. Infosys is a stepping stone, not a destination — plan your exit within 2 years.

Wipro WILP (Work Integrated Learning Program) test or standard campus placement. Online test followed by technical interview and HR. Wipro also has Elite and Turbo tracks with higher packages. Wipro Turbo track (6.5 LPA) needs medium DSA. Standard track (3.5 LPA) is basic aptitude and CS fundamentals. Elite track (4.5 LPA) is in between. Standard aptitude + reasoning + coding. Two coding problems, easy-medium. Wipro is not known for hard technical assessment. Very low CGPA (below 6) disqualifies you from some tracks. Not being able to write basic code in any language. Wipro is a good safety net but don't make it your primary target. Target Elite or Turbo tracks if you have the coding chops. Prepare OOPS, DBMS basics, and your project explanations alongside basic DSA.

Cognizant CCAT (Cognizant Core Aptitude Test) or campus placement. Cognizant GenC Elevate (8 LPA) and GenC Next (highest) are the premium tracks worth targeting. GenC Elevate and Next require stronger coding and CS fundamentals. Standard GenC is basic. Cognizant increasingly tests practical coding as they've improved their tech capabilities. Aptitude + coding. GenC Next has harder coding problems — LeetCode medium level. Standard GenC is much easier. Applying for standard GenC when you could qualify for Elevate — know your target track and prepare accordingly. Target GenC Next if you can — there's a significant package difference. Follow Cognizant's career page for test dates. Aptitude preparation is non-negotiable for service companies — mock tests on IndiaBIX and PrepInsta help.

Capgemini Pseudo-code test, English communication assessment, followed by technical + HR interview. Capgemini's process is unique with the pseudo-code test instead of actual coding. Capgemini tests logical thinking through pseudo-code, communication skills heavily, and project knowledge. Not a hard technical interview — communication matters as much as technical ability. Pseudo-code section is unusual — you read algorithmic pseudo-code and trace through it or answer questions. Practice this specifically since it's not like standard coding. Poor English communication is a real filter at Capgemini since they have large client-facing operations. Weak pseudo-code tracing. Practice Capgemini's specific pseudo-code question format from PrepInsta — it's genuinely different from other tests. Communication preparation matters here more than DSA prep.

Accenture ATAP (Accenture's online test): cognitive + technical + coding. Then technical interview and HR. Accenture ASE role is the standard; Advanced Technology roles have harder technical requirements. Accenture tests basic to medium technical knowledge. Advanced Technology roles need proper DSA. Standard ASE is mostly fundamentals and project explanation. Cognitive ability section is unique and must be practiced specifically. Technical MCQs cover OOPS, DBMS, networking basics. Coding section has 2 easy-medium problems. Not preparing for the cognitive section — many students fail here despite being technically strong. The cognitive portion is genuinely different from DSA. Practice Accenture's specific cognitive test format — it involves pattern recognition, fluid reasoning, and abstract thinking. You'll find past questions on PrepInsta. Balance cognitive prep with technical prep — both matter equally here.

Zomato Zomato's process is startup-like — fast, 2-3 rounds, focused on speed and execution. OA with 2 problems, then 1-2 technical rounds, then culture fit. Zomato moves fast — expect next-day callbacks if they like you. Delivery optimization, restaurant discovery, real-time systems. Zomato cares about practical problem-solving speed. They want people who can build and ship quickly. Medium-hard problems, 2 questions. Zomato has used unique problem formats in the past — read the problem statement carefully. Not being able to talk about tradeoffs, being too slow in coding rounds, or not showing enthusiasm for the food delivery domain. Target Zomato if you're comfortable with medium-hard DSA. Their packages are competitive. More importantly, show genuine excitement about their mission — culture fit matters at startups more than at services companies.

Ola OA then 2-3 technical rounds. Ola's interview is similar to other unicorns — practical DSA, some system design, domain relevance. Ola Electric roles have a different flavor than Ola Cabs. Routing algorithms, driver-rider matching, real-time location tracking. Graphs and geospatial algorithms come up in design discussions. Practically, medium DSA is sufficient for most roles. Standard 2-problem OA. Medium difficulty. Nothing surprising. Weak OOP and system design for senior roles. For fresher roles, not being able to solve medium DSA problems cleanly. Ola has gone through layoffs and pivots — research the team you're interviewing for (Ola Electric vs Ola Cabs vs OlaExperts). Packages are competitive but job security has been less stable than at larger companies.

Paytm OA, then 2-3 rounds technical + HR. Paytm has faced business challenges but still hires in their core payments and financial services verticals. Financial systems, payments, KYC flows. Similar fintech domain knowledge as PhonePe and Razorpay. Technical bar is somewhat lower than the tier-1 product companies. Medium difficulty, 2 problems. Standard format. Not knowing basic fintech concepts given their domain, and not researching Paytm's current business status before interviewing. Paytm's package and stability have been concerns — do your research before accepting. However it's still good experience and a stepping stone. Medium DSA prep is sufficient.

Byju's Byju's hiring for engineering roles involves OA and 2-3 technical rounds. Their engineering org (Whitehat Jr, Byju's core) has reduced in size significantly due to business issues. EdTech systems: video streaming, adaptive learning, content management. Standard product engineering role requirements — DSA, system design. Standard OA. Medium difficulty. Byju's has had significant financial issues — be very cautious about joining. Research current news before the interview. I'll be honest with you — Byju's has been through massive layoffs and salary delays. Target this only as a last resort or if the specific team is in a healthy business unit. The engineering experience can still be valuable but the risk is high.

Adobe Adobe has one of the most structured processes: OA, then 3-4 rounds including 1 DSA coding, 1 project-focused discussion, 1 LLD, and 1 system design + HR. Adobe hires heavily from IITs but also from strong NITs and top tier-2 colleges. Adobe tests DSA rigorously and also cares about OOP design — they make creative software and care about API design and extensibility. Expect hard coding questions alongside design questions. Adobe's OA has 2 hard coding problems. This is a real filter — many candidates are eliminated here. Prepare for hard LeetCode problems. Not being able to solve hard problems in coding rounds, or showing poor understanding of object-oriented design patterns in LLD. Adobe is achievable from tier-2 colleges — I've seen it happen. Their packages are excellent (25-35 LPA for freshers from IITs, slightly less from NITs and tier-2). Prepare hard LeetCode problems plus design patterns. Adobe recruits through GATE score sometimes for some roles — check both paths.

Oracle Oracle has a longer process — OA, then 3-5 technical rounds, then HR. They hire for specific teams (cloud, database, Java) and each team's process differs somewhat. Oracle cares deeply about database fundamentals (their core product), Java and OOP, and system design. For cloud roles, OS and networking concepts are tested. Oracle's OA has coding + MCQs on CS fundamentals. They sometimes test SQL alongside coding — know your SQL well. Weak database fundamentals (normalization, ACID, indexing, query optimization) when interviewing for a database company. Not knowing core Java features. Oracle is often overlooked in favor of trendier companies but their packages are excellent and job stability is very high. Strong DSA + DBMS fundamentals + Java OOP is the winning combination. Target Oracle for long-term career stability.

Atlassian Atlassian is known for a thorough and candidate-friendly process — OA, technical phone screen, then 3-4 onsite rounds including coding, system design, and a Values interview. They're headquartered in Australia with large India offices. Atlassian values code quality as much as algorithmic performance. Clean, maintainable code is a real evaluation criterion. They test medium-hard DSA and strong LLD/system design. Atlassian's Karat-conducted phone screens are common. Medium difficulty DSA problems with focus on code quality. Writing clever but unreadable code. Not following their values (team over self, build with heart and balance) in the Values interview. Atlassian actively recruits from India and the engineering culture is excellent. Their Values interview is serious — prepare specific examples showing collaboration, inclusion, and long-term thinking. Package is strong and work-life balance is better than FAANG.

Intuit Intuit's India offices (Bengaluru) have a well-defined process: OA, then 3 rounds — coding, system design, and a behavioral round. They make financial software (TurboTax, QuickBooks) and domain knowledge helps. Intuit tests medium-hard DSA, system design for financial applications, and cares strongly about product thinking. They want engineers who understand the customer problem, not just the technical solution. Standard 2-problem OA, medium difficulty. Clear and fair assessment. Not showing curiosity about the product or customer. Intuit expects engineers to think about users, not just algorithms. Intuit Bengaluru is genuinely a great place to work — good package, good culture, and meaningful work. Their product sense interview is real — read about personal finance problems in India before the interview. Medium LeetCode prep plus product thinking wins here.

Goldman Sachs (Tech) Goldman Sachs technology division (Marcus, platform engineering) has a rigorous process: HireVue video interview, then 3-4 technical rounds with strong emphasis on coding and a quantitative reasoning component. GS tech tests DSA rigorously and also tests probability, statistics, and mathematical problem-solving — more than most pure tech companies. Low-latency systems knowledge is valued for trading platform roles. Hackerrank OA with 2-3 coding problems plus aptitude. Their coding problems can be deceptively tricky with mathematical components. Weak quantitative reasoning. Not understanding financial products at a conceptual level (what is a derivative, what is a bond) for technology roles that support trading. GS tech in Bengaluru and Hyderabad pays extremely well (40-50 LPA+ at IIT-equivalent level). They also hire well from strong tier-2 colleges for specific roles. Medium-hard DSA plus probability/statistics preparation (Brilliant.org helps) is the combo.

Startups — Early Stage (Series A/B) Early-stage startup interviews are informal compared to big companies — 2-3 rounds, often including a take-home assignment or pair programming session. The founder or CTO often interviews directly. Startups care about breadth and the ability to learn fast. They want someone who can own a feature end-to-end. DSA matters less than system design and product thinking at early-stage companies. Many early startups skip OAs entirely and go to direct technical interviews or take-home projects. If they give an OA, it's usually a take-home with 2-3 hours to complete. Not showing ownership mentality, too focused on role boundaries ('that's not my job'), or being overspecialized in one area when they need generalists. For early-stage startups, your project portfolio matters as much as DSA. Build something real and be able to walk through technical decisions. LinkedIn is the best channel — warm introductions work well. Research the startup thoroughly before the interview — founders notice this.

Unacademy Unacademy's engineering interview process: OA, then 2-3 technical rounds. EdTech-specific domain questions about content delivery, live streaming, and personalization come up. Unacademy's core tech is content delivery and personalization. Video streaming, real-time interaction (live classes), and recommendation systems are relevant domains. Standard medium DSA for coding rounds. Standard 2-problem OA. Medium difficulty. Not researching Unacademy's significant financial challenges and business pivots — going in naive about their current situation looks bad. Like Byju's, research current news carefully before joining Unacademy. The edtech sector in India has contracted significantly. Target the role for experience and skills, but have a plan to move on in 1-2 years.

Freshers General — Tier 2/3 Colleges Most tier 2/3 colleges have a common placement process: OA on campus, then selected students go to company-specific interviews. Off-campus processes are different — direct OA links, then multiple virtual rounds. For tier 2/3 students, the OA is the biggest filter. Companies set a cutoff on OA scores before inviting students for interviews. Clearing the OA consistently is the first mission. Most companies use HackerEarth, HackerRank, or Codility for OAs. The difficulty is medium for product companies and easy-medium for service companies. Not practicing under timed conditions — OA timing pressure is real and many students who know the solution can't finish in time without practice. Practice OAs on HackerEarth and HackerRank under timed conditions weekly from 6th semester onwards. Speed matters. Submit even partial solutions — partial test case passes give partial marks. Don't skip aptitude prep — it's the difference between clearing and not clearing at service companies.

Microsoft MSFTIC (Research) Microsoft Research India (Bangalore) and MSFTIC have a different process than Microsoft's SDE hiring. Research positions require a research background. MSFTIC internship involves a research proposal. For research roles: publications or strong research projects, algorithmic depth, and mathematical maturity. For SDE in MSR India, similar to standard Microsoft SDE but with more algorithm-heavy questions. Research internship applications are portfolio-based, not OA-based. Regular SDE roles have standard OA. Applying to research roles without any research experience or strong interest in a specific problem area. MSR India is worth targeting if you have research interests — it opens doors to PhDs abroad and to unique engineering problems. Contact MSR researchers on LinkedIn with genuine questions about their work. Cold applications without networking rarely work for research roles.

Juspay Juspay is a Bangalore-based payments company focused on checkout infrastructure. Their interview is technical and deep — 3-4 rounds with strong emphasis on functional programming (they use Haskell and PureScript). Juspay is unique in the Indian startup ecosystem — they value functional programming, type systems, and mathematical thinking over conventional OOP. Their tech stack is unusual. Juspay's OA may include functional programming questions or unusual algorithmic problems. Be prepared for problems outside standard LeetCode territory. Not being open to learning unconventional tech stacks. Juspay attracts a specific type of engineer who enjoys functional programming. If you're interested in functional programming and unique engineering problems, Juspay is an excellent company. Learn Haskell basics or at least understand pure functions, immutability, and higher-order functions. Package is competitive and the engineering reputation is excellent.

Dream11 Dream11 (sports fantasy) has a focused interview process — OA, then 2-3 technical rounds. Their scale is remarkable (100M+ users during IPL) and system design questions reflect this. Real-time systems, high-traffic event handling, contest management. They face unique problems during live sports events when millions join contests simultaneously. Distributed systems knowledge is crucial. Standard 2-problem OA, medium-hard difficulty. Dream11 also uses take-home assignments for some roles. Not being able to discuss high-traffic system design. Dream11's traffic patterns are extreme — 10x normal load during match start. Dream11 has excellent engineering problems and a good engineering culture. Packages are competitive. Understanding their specific challenges (fantasy points calculation, real-time leaderboards, burst traffic) helps in design rounds. Research their tech blog before the interview.

Uber (India) Uber India's engineering hires go through a similar process to US: OA, then 4-5 rounds including coding, system design, and behavioral. Uber's India team works on global products. Routing, pricing algorithms (surge pricing), and real-time matching. Graphs and geospatial algorithms are genuinely relevant. System design for global scale. Uber's OA is on HackerRank with 2 medium-hard problems. Clean and well-defined. Not being able to scale your system design globally — Uber India builds for worldwide use, not just India. Uber pays exceptionally well (one of the highest in the market) and the problems are interesting. Targeting Uber requires strong DSA (LeetCode hard is not unusual) and solid system design. Not the first target for tier-2 students — build experience first, then aim here.

Google India (SWE, APM) Google India (Hyderabad, Bangalore) hires both SWEs and APMs. The APM (Associate Product Manager) program is separately targeted. SWE process is identical to global Google. APM has case studies and product design rounds. For APM: product sense, market sizing, metric definition, product design. For SWE: same as regular Google SWE — algorithms, data structures, coding clarity. APM uses a different application and screening process — resume screened first, then phone screen with product case, then onsite. For APM: not using structured frameworks (AARRR, CIRCLES) in product answers. For SWE: same as regular Google red flags. Google's APM program is highly competitive and coveted. If you're interested in product management from a CS background, this is one of the best entry points. Prepare with 'Cracking the PM Interview' and practice product cases with peers.

PayPal India PayPal Hyderabad and Chennai have a structured 3-4 round process. Strong fintech domain, OA then technical rounds. PayPal India works on global payments infrastructure. Payment processing, fraud detection, checkout flows. Financial systems knowledge (similar to other fintechs). Medium-hard DSA for coding rounds. Standard 2-3 problem OA. PayPal sometimes includes SQL or system design MCQs in the OA alongside coding. Weak understanding of security concepts (encryption, authentication) given PayPal's fraud detection work. PayPal India pays well and offers exposure to global financial infrastructure. Medium-hard DSA prep plus basic understanding of payment security (PCI compliance, tokenization) gives you an edge. Chennai office has a strong culture.

Walmart Global Tech India Walmart Global Tech Bangalore and Chennai: OA, then 3-4 rounds (coding, LLD, HLD, cultural interview). Walmart GCIT is one of the largest tech employers in India. Retail supply chain, inventory management, real-time inventory. Data engineering is huge at Walmart. Standard medium DSA for coding. Standard 2-problem OA on HackerEarth or similar. Medium difficulty. Sometimes includes MCQs on CS fundamentals. Not knowing basic system design or being unfamiliar with distributed databases and data warehousing concepts. Walmart Global Tech is often underrated. Package is good, work is meaningful (they power one of the world's largest retailers), and there's room for growth. Less glamorous than FAANG but a solid career choice.

Freshworks Freshworks (Chennai-based SaaS) has 3-4 rounds: OA, technical round with coding, LLD, and a cultural fit round. They're a Chennai-based success story with a strong engineering team. SaaS product engineering, APIs, multi-tenancy. Freshworks products are CRM, helpdesk, ITSM — domain knowledge helps. Medium DSA for technical rounds. Standard 2-problem OA. Medium difficulty. Freshworks sometimes includes problem-solving aptitude alongside coding. Not understanding SaaS concepts (multi-tenancy, subscription billing, integrations). Not being able to design clean REST APIs. Freshworks is an excellent company from a Chennai engineering hub perspective. Package is competitive, culture is good, and the SaaS domain is growing. If you're based in Chennai or don't want to move to Bengaluru, Freshworks is top tier. Target them seriously.

Nutanix India Nutanix Pune is a systems-heavy company. Their process: OA with strong systems questions, then 3-4 rounds covering OS, networking, distributed systems, and coding. Hyperconverged infrastructure, distributed storage, virtualization. This is systems programming territory — C, C++, kernel knowledge is valued. Not a web dev company. Nutanix's OA has systems-focused questions alongside coding. OS and networking MCQs are not unusual. Not having any systems programming background or interest. Nutanix is not the right target if you only want web development. Nutanix pays exceptionally well and the engineering problems are deep. If you enjoy OS, storage, and distributed systems — target Nutanix early and hard. Their packages rival FAANG. Study 'Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces' (free online) as preparation.

Build a solid foundation in basic data structures and write your first 50 programs without looking at solutions. Start with arrays, strings, and basic sorting — not linked lists yet. Pick one language and stick to it (Python if you want speed, Java or C++ if you plan to go deep). Spend the first week on problems where you already know the concept — easy LeetCode on arrays and strings. Code for 2 hours every day minimum, even if you only solve one problem. Use GeeksforGeeks for theory, LeetCode for practice. Don't jump to dynamic programming or graphs in month 1 — you need intuition before advanced topics. Don't watch YouTube solutions before genuinely attempting a problem for 30+ minutes. By day 30 you should be able to write array and string solutions without looking them up, and explain time/space complexity for your own code.

Master linked lists, stacks, queues, and basic tree traversals. Reach 100 problems solved with most solutions being original. Linked lists — reversal, cycle detection, merge — practice until you can code them without thinking. Then stacks and queues with implementation from scratch. End the month with binary trees: inorder, preorder, postorder, level order, height, and diameter. 2 new problems per day minimum. Don't skip the implementation-from-scratch exercises — building a linked list from raw memory gives you intuition that LeetCode alone doesn't. Don't move to the next topic before being comfortable with the current one. At 60 days, you should solve any easy LeetCode problem in under 20 minutes and feel comfortable with medium problems on arrays, strings, and basic trees.

Crack graphs, recursion, and start dynamic programming basics. Reach 150+ problems solved with growing confidence on mediums. Graphs are the most important topic for interviews — spend 3 weeks here. BFS, DFS, topological sort, connected components, and shortest paths (Dijkstra). Then recursion and backtracking: subsets, permutations, combination sum. End with 1D DP: fibonacci, climbing stairs, house robber, coin change. Don't try to learn graph algorithms passively by watching videos — you have to code them. Don't attempt hard DP before you're solid on medium DP. By day 90, you should solve medium graph and basic DP problems independently. Time yourself — target 25-35 minutes per medium problem.

Identify and fix your specific weak areas rather than reviewing things you already know. Targeted gap filling. Take LeetCode's 75 problem list and time yourself on every problem. Mark problems you couldn't solve or took over 45 minutes. Those are your gaps. Spend this month exclusively on those gap areas. Don't waste time on topics you're already solid on. Don't start from scratch — you're wasting time reviewing easy material. Also don't try to do everything at once; focused practice on weak areas outperforms scattered broad practice. After 30 days, redo the problems you couldn't solve at the start. You should be able to solve at least 70% of them now independently.

Reach 250+ problems solved including 50+ mediums and start mock interviews. Be interview-ready for service and mid-tier product companies. Add 2D DP, advanced graph algorithms, and binary search variants. Do 1 mock interview per week — either with a friend or on Pramp/Interviewing.io. Simulate real interview conditions: 45 minutes, camera on, talking while coding. Start applying to service companies as practice from month 2. Don't only practice easy and medium — attempt at least 5 hard problems per week to stretch your thinking. Don't skip the mock interview habit even when it feels uncomfortable. You're on track when you can consistently solve mediums in under 30 minutes and occasionally crack hard problems with hints.

Push from medium to hard comfort, start system design basics, and reach 300+ problems solved with a focus on common interview patterns. Categorize your LeetCode practice by pattern — sliding window, two pointer, monotonic stack, top K elements, tree DP, graph shortest paths. Solve 10 problems per pattern until pattern recognition becomes automatic. Start system design: read 'System Design Interview' by Alex Xu, practice designing URL shortener, rate limiter, and notification service. Don't just grind random problems — pattern-based practice is 3x more efficient for interview prep. Don't ignore system design even if your target companies don't explicitly require it — it makes you a stronger candidate. At 60 days, you should be able to identify the correct approach for most LeetCode medium problems within 3 minutes of reading the problem statement.

Be ready for product company interviews. Start applying to mid-tier product companies and use those as practice before targeting dream companies. Attempt 3 mock interviews per week, at least one with a stranger (Pramp or Interviewing.io). Solve the company-specific tagged problems on LeetCode for your target companies. Practice explaining your approach out loud — this is a skill and needs deliberate practice. Apply to companies in waves: service companies first for confidence, then mid-tier products, then top products and FAANG. Don't wait until you feel 'ready' to apply — you'll never feel 100% ready. Applications are practice. Don't neglect behavioral preparation — many strong coders lose offers because of poor STAR answers. You're ready to seriously apply when you're solving mediums in under 25 minutes and can explain your approach clearly while coding.

Target the 30-40 hard problems that appear most commonly at FAANG. Achieve consistent performance on hard problems within time constraints. Curate a list of FAANG-specific hard problems from LeetCode's company tags. Focus on hard DP (interval DP, bitmask DP), advanced graph problems (SCC, minimum spanning tree variations), and complex string problems. Do timed simulations of full interview rounds: 45 minutes, one hard or two mediums. Review solutions even for problems you solved to see alternate approaches. Don't spend time on obscure data structures like AVL trees or B-trees — they're rarely tested. Don't optimize for problem count; optimize for depth of understanding on the problems you do practice. You're ready for FAANG rounds when you can solve most mediums in under 20 minutes and have a reasonable attempt at hard problems within 40 minutes.

Switch from learning mode to interview mode. Stop learning new topics and focus entirely on consolidation, speed, and communication. Redo all the problems you've solved before — can you solve them faster now? Start 3 mock interviews per week minimum. Research each target company's OA pattern and do 5 practice OAs under time pressure. Update your resume this week — don't leave it for the last minute. Apply to companies whose applications are already open. Don't start learning new advanced topics 60 days before placements — consolidate what you know. Don't neglect OA preparation — many students fail OA because they practice problems without timing. You're on track if you're clearing mock OAs consistently and can discuss your projects confidently for 10+ minutes without preparation.

Peak preparation — maximum sharpness on DSA, clear system design understanding, and polished behavioral answers. Daily practice: 2 medium problems (timed, 25 min each), 1 system design concept review, 1 STAR story practice. Review your top 20 most important DSA patterns and confirm you can code each cleanly. Practice coding on paper or a whiteboard at least twice — some companies still use whiteboards. Make sure your LinkedIn is updated. Don't cram new topics in the last 30 days — every hour spent learning something new takes away from consolidating what you know. Don't neglect sleep in the final weeks — cognitive performance degrades badly with sleep deprivation. 30 days out, you should be scoring 7+ out of 10 on mock interview sessions. If consistently below 5, identify the specific weakness and address it immediately.

Polish, not panic. Review, don't learn. Rest enough to perform at your best. Solve 2 easy-medium problems each morning to stay sharp — don't attempt hard problems this week. Review your key patterns: sliding window, two pointer, tree DFS/BFS, graph BFS/Dijkstra, basic DP. Read the company's tech blog and recent news. Write down your 5 STAR stories on paper and practice saying them aloud. Plan your logistics — location, time zone if virtual, internet connection. Don't solve 10 hard problems the night before — it creates anxiety, not readiness. Don't make major changes to your resume this close to the interview. The night before, you should feel moderately nervous (healthy) but not panicked. If you've prepared consistently, trust it — last-minute cramming doesn't change outcomes.

Active rest and final mental preparation. Preserve cognitive sharpness, not increase knowledge. Solve only 1-2 easy problems in the morning to warm up your brain. Review your STAR stories once. Look up the interviewer on LinkedIn if you know who it is — find common ground. Sleep by 10 PM. Prepare your setup if it's a virtual interview: test your mic, camera, internet, and IDE. Have water ready and remove distractions. Don't study for more than 2 hours total. Don't start a new LeetCode problem after 6 PM the day before. Don't skip dinner — blood sugar affects cognitive performance measurably. Success metric for the day before is waking up the day of the interview feeling rested and focused, not exhausted and anxious.

Build genuine technical foundation and first internship, not just placement prep. This is different from 3rd-year prep — you have time to build real skills. Learn one web framework deeply (Django or Node.js) and build 2-3 full projects. Solve 100 LeetCode problems across easy and medium — no pressure for competitive programming yet. Open source contributions: find a beginner-friendly project on GitHub and make 3-5 pull requests. These activities open the internship door and build your resume. Don't focus only on competitive programming in 2nd year — you need projects for internship applications. Don't neglect your CGPA below 7 if you want FAANG — some have a cutoff. At 6 months, you should have 1 deployed project, 100+ LeetCode problems, and a basic GitHub profile that shows consistent commits.

Land a meaningful summer internship (preferably paid, product company or startup) that converts to PPO or becomes strong resume material. December-January: apply aggressively to internships (on-campus + off-campus). Start LeetCode medium seriously. February-March: interview preparation peaks. April-May: internship begins. During internship: work hard, communicate proactively, and explicitly tell your manager you're interested in a PPO in month 1. Don't apply to only 5-10 companies — apply to 50+ for internships. The funnel is huge. Don't wait for campus placements to start — the best internships are off-campus. Getting at least 3 technical interview calls from internship applications by February shows you're on track. Fewer than 3 means your resume needs work.

Complete the learning phase, build the resume, and position yourself optimally for placement season. Finish all major DSA topics by month 6 before placements — no new topics after that. Build or polish a significant project that can be the centerpiece of your resume. If you have a gap in your resume (no internship, low CGPA), address it with a strong project or open source contribution now. Don't leave resume building for the last month — it takes 2-3 weeks to get it right. Don't ignore CGPA constraints for companies you're targeting. 6 months before placements, your DSA should be solid enough that you can solve mediums on any topic. If specific areas are weak, you still have time to fix them.

Maximum intensity preparation combining DSA revision, mock interviews, and resume finalization simultaneously. Daily schedule: morning 2 medium problems (60 min), afternoon 1 system design concept, evening 1 STAR story practice. Get your resume reviewed by at least 2 seniors this week. Apply to companies whose processes are already open. Start company-specific preparation for your top 5 targets. Don't try to learn new DSA topics from scratch — there's no time. Focus on solidifying patterns you know. Don't over-prepare behavioral and under-prepare technical or vice versa. In 30 days (30 days before placements), you should be able to clear a mock OA at your target company's difficulty level 7 out of 10 times.

Convert all preparation into interview-ready performance. No new content — only refinement and simulation. Mock interviews every other day minimum. Review company-specific patterns for your top targets. Practice writing code cleanly under pressure — your handwriting or typing should be clear even when nervous. Confirm your resume is final and reviewed. Set up interview logistics — quiet room, good internet, proper lighting for video. Don't attempt LeetCode hard problems you've never seen before — it's demoralizing right before placements. Stick to familiar patterns. Two weeks out, you should feel 'prepared but excited' not 'prepared and relieved.' If you feel relieved it's over, you probably under-prepared.

Analyze failures, fix specific gaps, and target the next wave of placements with a better approach. After a failed interview, write down within 24 hours: exactly which question stumped you and why, what you wish you'd said in behavioral rounds, where you felt the interviewer's energy shift negatively. This post-mortem is gold. Fix one specific weakness per week. Keep applying — placement season has multiple waves. Don't generalize 'I'm bad at DSA' from one failed interview — diagnose specifically. Also don't wait weeks between applications to 'prepare more' — applying and failing is itself preparation. If you've had 5+ failed technical interviews without an offer, do a serious pattern analysis. One failure type (OA, DSA round, behavioral) should dominate — that's your fixable issue.

Target FAANG and top product companies with surgical preparation — not broad grinding but targeted excellence. NeetCode 150 is not enough for FAANG — solve company-specific premium problems on LeetCode for Google, Meta, Amazon. Do full system design mock sessions (60 min, design Instagram/Uber/Twitter) with a peer who gives honest feedback. Write about what you build — a tech blog helps with FAANG visibility. Target referrals through LinkedIn — 80% of FAANG offers come from referrals. Don't apply to FAANG cold without a referral if you have any way to get one — referral vs. cold application success rates are dramatically different. Don't skip the system design preparation even if you're a fresher. You're FAANG-ready when you can solve 2 mediums in 45 minutes with clean code and clear explanation, AND design a scalable system with appropriate trade-offs in 45 minutes.

Get placement-ready as fast as possible for service companies and entry-level product companies. Focused, no wasted time. Week 1-2: arrays and strings only, 3 easy problems per day. Week 3-4: stacks, queues, basic linked lists. Month 2: trees (BST, BFS, DFS), basic recursion. Month 3: graphs (BFS/DFS only, no advanced), basic DP (fibonacci, climbing stairs, coin change). Simultaneously, prepare CS fundamentals: OOPS, DBMS, OS basics for technical interviews at service companies. Don't attempt medium problems until you're fluid on easy problems. Don't spend time on competitive programming at this stage — you need placement-focused preparation, not competitive programming. At 90 days, you should clear service company OAs consistently and have a reasonable chance at mid-tier product company OAs. If you can't clear TCS/Infosys OAs, review your basics.

3rd or 4th year students with no internship experience building their first serious resume. Your project needs to solve a real problem, not just implement a tutorial. Recruiters can tell the difference between 'I followed a YouTube tutorial' and 'I built something because I needed it.' The single best project type for freshers is a web app with a backend API, database, and deployment — it shows end-to-end capability. Make it something you can talk about for 10 minutes without notes — your projects are the centerpiece of your fresher interview. Instead of 'To-Do app with React,' build 'College notice board where students can subscribe to departments and get WhatsApp notifications for announcements' — same tech, real problem at your college, much stronger story.

Students who have done multiple small projects and want to decide which to show. Two good projects beat five mediocre ones every time. Pick your two strongest projects and polish them completely — deployed, documented, clean GitHub code, and a README that explains what it does, why you built it, and what you learned. Don't list 5 projects where 3 of them are half-finished — it signals low follow-through. Quality matters far more than quantity in a fresher resume. One project: 'Expense tracker web app — deployed on Railway, 200 users from my college use it daily, built with Django and PostgreSQL.' This one project tells a better story than five tutorial clones.

Students who have completed a paid internship at a product company or startup. Your internship project is your #1 resume item — it outweighs any personal projects you've built. Quantify your internship impact specifically: lines of code means nothing, but 'reduced API response time by 40%' or 'feature used by 10,000 users' means everything. If you can't quantify it, describe the business impact. Explain your specific contribution, not the team's work. 'Implemented Redis caching layer for product search API, reducing average response time from 800ms to 120ms, directly improving the checkout conversion rate for 50,000 daily active users.'

Any Indian CS student submitting to product or service companies. Single page, always, for freshers. No exceptions. The Jake's Resume template on Overleaf is the gold standard — clean, ATS-friendly, single column, good whitespace. No profile photo (it introduces unconscious bias and wastes space). No objective statement (waste of space). Order: Education, Experience/Internship, Projects, Skills, Achievements. Keep it reverse chronological. Font: Garamond or Calibri 10-11pt. Margins: 0.5 inch. Section headers slightly larger. Bullet points start with strong action verbs (Developed, Implemented, Optimized, Reduced).

Students unsure how to write bullet points for their experience and projects. Every bullet point should follow this structure: [Action verb] + [What you did] + [With what technology] + [Result/Impact]. Weak: 'Worked on backend.' Strong: 'Developed REST API for user authentication using Node.js and JWT, handling 5,000 requests per day.' If you don't have metrics, estimate or describe the context. 'Used by 200 students at my college' is a metric. 'Reduced manual work by 3 hours per week' is a metric. Before: 'Made database queries faster.' After: 'Optimized 3 critical SQL queries by adding composite indexes and rewriting N+1 queries, reducing average page load time by 60%.'

Students building their GitHub profile for resume and recruiter visibility. Recruiters look at 3 things on your GitHub: your profile README, your pinned repositories, and your contribution graph. Pin your 4-6 best projects, write a clear README for each one (problem it solves, tech stack, screenshots, how to run it), and maintain consistent commits. A green contribution graph signals active development. Don't pin repositories with a single commit from a year ago. Your GitHub README should have: who you are (2 lines), what you're currently working on, your tech stack as badges, and 3-4 links to your best projects. This takes 1 hour to set up and makes a huge impression.

Students building the skills section of their resume. Don't list every technology you've ever touched. Only list technologies you can answer questions about in an interview. Recruiters and interviewers will ask about anything on your resume — if you put 'Machine Learning' in skills and can't explain gradient descent, that's a red flag. Organize by category: Languages, Frameworks, Databases, Tools. Don't include MS Office or basic typing speed. Languages: Python, Java, JavaScript. Frameworks: Django, React, Node.js. Databases: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis. Tools: Git, Docker, AWS (EC2, S3). This is clean and defensible.

Students with low CGPA (below 7.0) wondering whether to put it on their resume. If your CGPA is below 7.0, most product companies will filter you at resume stage — it's a hard cutoff in their ATS. You have two options: work to improve it if you're still in earlier semesters, or compensate with a very strong project and internship section. If it's above 7.0 but not great, list it but draw attention away from it with strong project bullets. If the company explicitly asks and your CGPA is low, be honest — lying is far worse. If your CGPA is 6.2 but you have a strong internship and 2 good projects, prioritize off-campus applications to startups and mid-tier companies that don't have hard CGPA cutoffs. Many good companies care more about what you've built than your GPA.

Students who have completed an internship writing about it on their resume. Your internship section should have: company name, role title, dates (month + year), location (Remote or city), and 3-4 bullet points. Each bullet should describe a specific contribution. Don't write 'Helped the team with development' — write what specifically you built. If you worked on a feature, name it. If you fixed bugs, say how many or which system. The internship is your biggest differentiator as a fresher. 'Software Engineering Intern, Razorpay (June-August 2024, Bangalore). Developed rate limiting middleware for the payment processing API using Redis, reducing fraudulent request rate by 15%.'

Students who want to add open source to their resume but don't know how. You don't need to contribute to a major project to list open source on your resume — any public contribution counts. Start by fixing documentation errors or typos (yes, this counts and gets you comfortable with the PR process). Then graduate to fixing 'good first issue' labeled bugs in tools you actually use. List contributions as: 'Contributed to [project] — fixed [what], merged into [version].' The act of contributing shows initiative beyond coursework. Find 'good first issue' labels on GitHub for projects you use: Django, React, VS Code extensions. A merged PR to any of these is legitimately impressive on a fresher resume.

Students who have built projects locally but haven't deployed them. A deployed project is worth 5x an undeployed one. Recruiters and interviewers want to see something live — it shows you understand the full development cycle. Deploy your web projects on Railway, Render, or Vercel (all have free tiers). Deploy your ML projects on HuggingFace Spaces. Don't say 'can share code on request' — share the live link and the GitHub link both. Deployment is not optional for serious resume projects. A simple Flask API deployed on Railway with a React frontend on Vercel takes 30 minutes to set up. Your resume says 'Live at: yourproject.railway.app' — this makes your project verifiable and impressive.

Students who have LinkedIn profiles but aren't getting recruiter attention. Your LinkedIn headline is more important than your summary. It should not say 'CS Student at XYZ College' — that tells recruiters nothing actionable. Instead: 'Backend Developer | Python, Django, AWS | Looking for SWE Internship 2025 | Building [your project].' Recruiters search by skills, not college names. Add all the skills you're comfortable defending. Connect with 5 seniors from your college who are at your target companies — their connections make you more visible. 'Backend Developer (Intern) | Django • PostgreSQL • Docker | Open to SWE Internships & FTE | Building expense tracking SaaS' — this headline gets more profile views than 'Student at SPCE, Mumbai'.

Students wondering whether they need a personal portfolio website. A portfolio website is optional for SWE roles but becomes important for UI/UX, frontend, and design-adjacent positions. For pure backend or full-stack SWE roles, a clean GitHub profile + LinkedIn is sufficient — don't spend 3 weeks building a portfolio when you could be solving LeetCode or building a better project. If you do build one, host it on GitHub Pages or Vercel, keep it simple, and make sure it loads fast. If you're a frontend-focused developer, a portfolio makes sense — but make it fast, minimal, and mobile-responsive. A portfolio that looks bad on mobile from a frontend dev is a red flag.

Students who want to understand how resumes are evaluated in Indian companies. In Indian companies, resumes go through two filters: ATS (automated keyword matching) and the recruiter (10-second human scan). The ATS filters on: keywords from the job description, CGPA cutoff, college tier. The recruiter scans for: known company names in experience, project descriptions with metrics, and overall cleanliness. Neither cares about your resume's design beyond basic readability. Match keywords from the job description in your skills and project descriptions. If the job description says 'RESTful APIs, microservices, Kubernetes,' and you have that experience, use those exact words in your resume — not synonyms. ATS matching is literal.

Students applying to companies that use ATS filtering (most mid to large companies). Use standard section headers: 'Experience', 'Education', 'Skills', 'Projects' — not creative names like 'What I've Built' or 'My Journey.' Use a single-column format — ATS struggles with multi-column layouts. Don't use tables, text boxes, or headers/footers for content. Don't put crucial information in graphics or images. PDF format is standard but some ATS prefer .docx — follow the job posting's instructions. Jake's Resume template on Overleaf is already ATS-optimized. If you're using a fancy Canva template with text in image boxes, switch immediately — your resume may not be parsing at all.

Students wondering what NOT to include in their resume. Remove these immediately: hobbies and interests (unless they're technical), 'references available on request,' your date of birth, marital status, religion, father's name, passport number, and home address. These are irrelevant and take up space. Also remove: 'Objective: To work in a dynamic company' — it's a filler. Every line on a single-page resume is precious — use it all for substance. The space you save by removing 'Hobbies: Reading, Cricket, Music' can be used for one more project bullet point with an actual metric — that's always the better choice.

Students in tier-2/3 colleges with no internship trying to compete against IIT students. Your project has to be your equalizer. An IIT student with a mediocre project and you with a genuinely impressive project — you win. 'Impressive' means deployed, solves a real problem, has users (even 10 users is users), and you understand every technical decision in it. Make one project really good instead of three average ones. Be able to answer 'why did you choose PostgreSQL over MySQL here?' confidently. A hostel food pre-ordering system for your own college canteen that 50 students actually use daily is infinitely more impressive than a perfectly cloned Netflix UI that exists only on localhost.

Students applying for ML/AI roles and data science positions. For ML roles, your skills need to show the stack: Python, NumPy/Pandas/Scikit-learn for classical ML; PyTorch or TensorFlow for deep learning; SQL for data manipulation. List any cloud ML platforms you've used: AWS SageMaker, Google Colab, HuggingFace. Don't list 'Machine Learning' as a standalone skill — break it down. 'Familiarity with transformer architectures and fine-tuning' is more specific and more credible. ML Skills section: 'Languages: Python. Libraries: PyTorch, Scikit-learn, Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib. Tools: Jupyter, HuggingFace, Weights & Biases, Google Colab. Concepts: CNNs, Transformers, Transfer Learning, XGBoost.'

High CGPA students wondering if they should emphasize it. A CGPA above 8.5 is genuinely useful to highlight — put it prominently. Above 9.0 is very impressive and worth mentioning in interviews too. However, high CGPA without projects or internship is a weaker resume than medium CGPA with strong experience. Don't let high CGPA make you lazy about building things — companies value builders. High CGPA opens doors, but projects and internship keep them open. 'B.Tech Computer Science, VJTI Mumbai | CGPA: 9.1/10 | Relevant Coursework: Operating Systems, Database Systems, Computer Networks' — the high CGPA warrants its own prominent line.

Students with unpaid or research-based internships. All internships belong on your resume — paid or unpaid, industry or research, 2 months or 6 months. A research internship under a professor is valuable, especially if it led to any publication, dataset, or system. An unpaid startup internship where you built real things is better than no internship. The key is what you built or learned, not whether you were paid. Research Intern, IIT Bombay (May-July 2024): 'Worked with Prof. [Name] on question-answering systems using BERT fine-tuning; implemented dataset curation pipeline processing 50,000 document pairs.'

Students applying for both Indian domestic and international (US/UK) companies. For Indian companies: single page, no photo, clean template — already covered. For international companies (US/UK): same rules apply — single page, no photo, no DOB, no marital status (illegal to ask in the US). US resumes are called 'resumes,' not 'CVs.' For UK and Europe, 2-page CVs are acceptable for experienced candidates but 1 page remains best practice for freshers. Keep two versions: one tuned to Indian company job descriptions with Indian context metrics, one with more global framing ('Built API handling 5,000 requests/day' works for both).

Students who want open source to be a major part of their profile. If you want open source to be a differentiator, you need sustained contributions over time, not one PR. GSoC (Google Summer of Code) is the gold standard for student open source — it pays (stipend), it's recognized globally, and it proves you can contribute to a real codebase for 3 months. Apply to GSoC in your pre-final year — the selection is competitive but not impossible from tier-2 colleges. GSoC student selected from tier-3 college with strong open source history beats IIT student with no contributions in a FAANG referral from the GSoC organization. This literally happens.

Students applying off-campus without a college placement cell doing the filtering. When you apply off-campus, your LinkedIn profile is seen before your resume. Recruiters search LinkedIn for candidates — if your profile doesn't rank for 'Python Developer Bangalore 2025' or similar, you won't be found. Your resume gets opened when your profile creates interest. Optimize LinkedIn first, then send your resume as confirmation of what they already liked on LinkedIn. Message pattern that works: connect on LinkedIn first with a personalized note, engage with their company content, then apply via the job posting. Cold applying without profile visibility has a ~2% response rate.

Students with messy GitHub profiles full of tutorial repos and failed experiments. Clean up your GitHub before sharing it. Archive old repos rather than deleting them. Unpin anything that's embarrassing (empty repos, 1-commit clones, todo apps with no README). Your 6 pinned repos are your portfolio — treat them like public product demos. Write READMEs with badges, screenshots, and setup instructions. A GitHub profile that looks like a graveyard of half-finished ideas sends the wrong signal. Pin only repos where: the README explains the project clearly, there's meaningful code (not just HTML/CSS from Week 1 tutorials), and ideally there's a live demo link or screenshots.

Students applying to large Indian IT companies and MNCs with structured ATS. Each job description tells you exactly which keywords to include in your resume. Read it carefully and mirror the exact terms they use. If they say 'RESTful web services,' use that phrase, not 'REST APIs.' If they say 'Agile methodology,' use that if it applies to you. Don't keyword stuff — match your actual experience to their language. This is not gaming the system; it's good communication. Job description says: 'Experience with cloud platforms (AWS/GCP/Azure).' Your resume should specifically mention: 'Deployed application on AWS EC2 with S3 for storage' — not just 'deployed on cloud.'

Students with 2+ internships writing about technical decisions in their resume. For students with strong experience, your resume can afford to go one layer deeper on technical decisions. Don't just say what you built — say why you made key technical choices. 'Chose Kafka over RabbitMQ for high-throughput event streaming' is more impressive than 'Used messaging queue.' This shows you can evaluate tradeoffs, which is a senior-engineering skill. Before: 'Built notification service.' After: 'Designed event-driven notification service using Kafka for decoupling, supporting 100k events/day; chose Kafka over Redis pub/sub for replay capability and stronger delivery guarantees.'

Students in their 2nd year trying to get noticed for early internships. In 2nd year, LinkedIn is more important than your resume because you have little to show — LinkedIn lets you tell your story in progress. Post about what you're learning: share that you solved your first dynamic programming problem, that you deployed your first API, that you contributed to open source. This activity makes you visible to recruiters and peers. People hire from LinkedIn connections — your college seniors can refer you before you have a traditional resume. Post: 'Finally understood Dijkstra's algorithm after struggling with it for a week! Here's the key insight that made it click for me [explanation + code snippet].' This post gets you noticed by people who hire.

Students debating whether to spend time on a portfolio vs. solving LeetCode. Time ROI: portfolio = low ROI for pure SWE roles, high ROI for design/frontend/creative roles. If you have 10 hours, put 8 on LeetCode and projects, 2 on LinkedIn optimization. Portfolio only makes sense if you have something to show — if your projects are all tutorial clones, a portfolio just makes that more visible. Build good projects first, portfolio is the packaging. A SWE fresher applying to Flipkart doesn't need a portfolio website. A UI/UX engineer applying to Swiggy definitely does — let the role guide the investment.

Students with achievements from competitive programming or hackathons. Competitive programming achievements belong prominently on your resume if they're meaningful: top 500 globally in Codeforces, CodeChef 5-star or above, ICPC Regionalist. LeetCode solve count does not go on your resume — it's not an achievement, it's preparation. Hackathon wins go in achievements: 'Won Best Technical Project at HackIIT 2024 (500+ participants).' These genuinely differentiate you. Achievements section: 'Codeforces Expert (max rating 1600). ICPC Asia Regionals participant 2023. Winner, Smart India Hackathon 2024 (Problem Statement 23, Ministry of Health).'

Students getting callbacks but failing at resume screening stage consistently. If you're consistently not getting callbacks, the issue is one of: CGPA below cutoff (fixable by applying to companies without hard cutoffs), resume not parsing in ATS (use single-column plain format), or your project descriptions are too vague (add metrics and specifics). Get someone who works at a product company to review your resume — not a placement cell coordinator. Real practitioners see thousands of resumes and can spot problems in seconds. Ask 2 college seniors who got placed at product companies to review your resume and give you blunt feedback. Offer to pay for their coffee. This 1-hour investment is worth more than reading 10 more resume guides.

Cold emailing works when your email is specific, short, and shows you've done your homework. Find the hiring manager or a team lead (not HR) on LinkedIn, get their email (usually firstname.lastname@company.com or firstname@company.com). Subject: 'Software Intern Application — [Your College], [Year], [Specific Skill].' Body: 3 sentences — who you are, one specific reason you're interested in their team/product, what you can offer. Link to GitHub, no attachment. Sending a generic cold email that could apply to any company — these go unread. The email must reference something specific about their product or team that shows you actually researched them. Getting a reply within 2 weeks, even if it's a 'we don't have openings now but check back' — that means your email was good. Response rate above 5% for cold emails is actually solid.

LinkedIn outreach is warmer than cold email because there's a human connection. The formula: connect with a note (50 words max), wait for acceptance, then send one follow-up message about why you're interested in their company specifically. Don't ask for jobs in the first message — ask for a 15-minute conversation to learn about their work. People help when asked for advice, not when asked for jobs. Sending a connection request with 'Hi, I'm looking for an internship, please help' — this gets ignored immediately. The ask has to come after establishing a genuine connection. Getting a 15-minute coffee chat. Even if it doesn't lead to a job, you get insights about the company and your name goes in their mental rolodex.

On-campus internship season typically runs from December to March for May-June internships. Register for placement portal early — some colleges have a CGPA cutoff for registration. Prepare your resume in November so it's ready when companies come. On-campus processes are more structured and often more accessible than off-campus — many companies specifically set aside slots for tier-2/3 colleges through placement partnerships. Missing the registration deadline for your campus placement portal — this happens every year to multiple students, and it's entirely avoidable. Set calendar reminders. Getting shortlisted from resume screening for at least 3 companies in your target tier — if you're not getting shortlisted, resume needs work before the main season.

Off-campus internships are where the best opportunities are — many top product companies don't visit tier-2/3 campuses but actively hire off-campus. LinkedIn Jobs, AngelList/Wellfound (for startups), Internshala, and direct company career pages are your channels. Apply to 30-50+ companies — the funnel is large. For startups, applying during their funding round is smart (they have hiring budget but need to move fast). Applying to only 5-10 companies off-campus and waiting for responses before applying more — the internship search is a numbers game in the early stages. Apply in batches of 20-30. Getting 3+ interview calls from 30 applications means your resume is working. Fewer than 3 from 30 means the resume needs fixing before applying more.

Early-stage startups are the most accessible internship opportunity for students without strong resumes yet. They're desperate for good engineers and less credential-focused. Find startups on AngelList, ProductHunt, LinkedIn, and YC's company directory. Cold email founders directly — they read their email. Propose a specific contribution: 'I noticed your app doesn't have dark mode — I can build and ship this in 2 weeks.' Specific value proposals work at startups. Looking only at well-known startups — the best learning opportunities and easiest access are at Series A/B startups with 20-100 employees, not unicorns. Getting a 'yes' from a startup even for an unpaid internship in 2nd year is a win — the experience and reference make 3rd year paid opportunities much more accessible.

PPO (Pre-Placement Offer) is the best outcome from an internship — it converts to full-time without going through placement season. To get a PPO: treat your internship like a 3-month job interview. Complete your assigned work faster than expected, identify and solve a problem no one asked you to solve, build relationships across the team (not just your manager), and explicitly say you want a PPO by week 4 — don't assume they'll offer without you expressing interest. Being silent about wanting a PPO and hoping the manager will bring it up — in most companies, PPO processes require the manager to proactively nominate you, and they nominate people who've expressed interest. Being given increasingly complex tasks as the internship progresses means you're performing well. Getting access to production codebases or being included in team meetings beyond interns is a very good sign.

Internship interviews are 60-70% as hard as full-time interviews at the same company. Medium LeetCode is the baseline for product company internship interviews. The technical process typically has one OA and one-two interview rounds. Behavioral questions are lighter — focus on 'tell me about a project you built' and 'why do you want to work here.' Prepare both technical and project explanation thoroughly. Over-preparing for internship interviews as if they're full-time interviews — medium DSA and solid project knowledge is genuinely sufficient for most internship processes. Don't over-invest here; use the time to apply to more places instead. Clearing 40-50% of internship OAs you attempt. If clearing fewer than 30%, DSA preparation needs work. If clearing OA but failing all technical interviews, project explanation and communication need work.

The first week is for understanding the codebase, team, and processes — don't code immediately, ask questions. Write down everything you learn. Week 2-3: start contributing to real tickets, not just test cases. Ask for code review on everything — the feedback is the most valuable learning. Keep a daily log of what you worked on — this becomes your resume bullet points and interview answers. Over-communicate progress with your manager. Being too shy to ask questions and struggling silently — senior engineers expect interns to ask questions. Silence is interpreted as either arrogance or disinterest, not self-sufficiency. When team members start coming to you with small questions related to your work area — that's when you know you've established credibility.

PPO conversion depends on: technical performance (70% weight), communication and attitude (20%), and team fit (10%). Technical performance means completing your project deliverables and going slightly beyond scope. Communication means proactively sharing updates, asking good questions in reviews, and being reliable. Team fit means people enjoy working with you — be helpful to other interns, participate in team culture. Focusing only on technical work and ignoring soft skills — many technically excellent interns don't get PPOs because they were hard to communicate with or didn't build relationships. Getting positive feedback in your mid-internship review and being told you'll have a final evaluation at the end — if your manager is vague about next steps at mid-point, ask directly about the PPO process.

Tier-2 college students need to start earlier and apply more than IIT students for the same internship opportunities. Start applying in November for summer internships — IIT students start in September with on-campus placements. Your advantage is flexibility and hustle. Many top product companies actively want to diversify beyond IITs — Razorpay, Meesho, Juspay, and others specifically recruit from tier-2 colleges. Don't self-reject before applying. Not applying to companies you think 'won't take tier-2 students' — many product companies have removed this bias. The worst outcome of applying is no response. Self-rejection before applying is the real career killer. Getting technical interviews from 1-2 product companies by February (for summer internships). Even one product company internship significantly changes your full-time placement outcomes.

No experience means you need to create evidence of skills instead of citing past work. Build one real project end-to-end and deploy it. Contribute to one open source project. Solve 75+ LeetCode problems. These three things together give you enough to apply for startup internships. You won't get FAANG with no experience, but you can get meaningful startup experience that becomes your foundation. Waiting to build projects until you 'know enough' — the perfect project is the enemy of the shipped project. Build something basic, deploy it, and iterate. Deployed > perfect. Having one deployed project with at least 10 GitHub commits and being able to explain every technical decision in it. That alone opens doors at early-stage startups.

2nd year internships are for learning and building credibility — unpaid is fine, startup is great, anything where you write real code matters. 3rd year internships should be paid and at a company whose name strengthens your resume. The 2nd year internship is investment, 3rd year is harvest. Don't skip 2nd year internship by thinking it's too early — the gap between students who interned early and those who didn't is massive by 4th year. Targeting FAANG in 2nd year instead of building skills through accessible startups — FAANG rejection in 2nd year can be demoralizing; startup success builds confidence and skills. Having one tangible experience (code in production, shipped feature, merged PR) by end of 2nd year summer. This one thing separates you from 80% of your peers in 3rd year applications.

Volume matters but personalization matters more. Send 20 personalized emails to specific engineers or managers at target companies rather than 100 generic emails to HR@company.com. Find engineering managers or tech leads on LinkedIn, find their email pattern, send a 4-sentence email: your background, specific interest in their work, what you've already built that's relevant, and a clear ask. Include your GitHub link in the email body, not as an attachment. Emailing only HR and assuming they'll forward to the right person — HR for internship roles at product companies is overwhelmed. Direct outreach to engineers gets 3-5x better response rates. One reply out of every 10-15 cold emails means your targeting and personalization are working. Below that, improve the email quality before sending more.

Before the internship ends, have an explicit conversation with your manager: 'I've really enjoyed this summer. I'd love to join full-time after graduation. What does the PPO process look like here?' This one conversation triggers the process. If they say they're not sure, ask what you could do in the remaining time to strengthen your case. This directness is respected, not seen as presumptuous. Leaving the internship without having this conversation and then being surprised when no PPO is extended — managers have to nominate interns for PPO, and they nominate those who asked. Manager says 'We'll start the PPO process' or 'HR will reach out about next steps' — that's a yes. Anything vague means follow up.

On-campus internships at top product companies (Flipkart, Razorpay, Meesho, Juspay) typically arrive at IITs first, then NITs, then tier-2 colleges with a 1-2 month delay. Use this delay strategically: prepare in September-November, apply to off-campus simultaneously in December, and use on-campus as a parallel track rather than your only plan. Waiting exclusively for on-campus companies to arrive and applying nowhere off-campus — the best internships at many companies are off-campus only. By the time on-campus season starts at tier-2 colleges, the best startups have already filled their summer cohorts. Having at least one off-campus offer in hand when on-campus season begins — it removes desperation from your on-campus interviews and gives you negotiating context.

For 3rd year, target funded startups in the Series A-C range — they have money, they're growing, and they'll give you real responsibilities fast. Y Combinator company directory, Tracxn, and Crunchbase show recently funded companies. Funded startups post on LinkedIn Jobs and AngelList. Reach out within 3 months of their funding announcement — that's when they have budget and are actively hiring. Targeting only well-known unicorn startups (Zepto, CRED) which are highly competitive, while ignoring the dozens of well-funded, 50-200 person startups that desperately need good engineers. A startup internship where you're the only intern or one of very few — this means you'll get real work, real responsibility, and a strong reference.

After connecting with someone senior at your target company, the highest-ROI message is: 'I'm preparing for interviews at [Company]. Would you be willing to share 15 minutes to tell me what the engineering culture is like and what made you join?' This is flattering, non-threatening, and very likely to get a response. In that conversation, mention your background. They will often offer to refer you if they're impressed — but don't ask for a referral directly. Asking for a referral in the first message — it's too soon and feels transactional. Build a micro-relationship first. Even two LinkedIn message exchanges before asking for a call changes the dynamic. The person says 'I'll forward your profile to our recruiter' without you having to ask — that's the best outcome of a LinkedIn conversation.

Internship platforms built specifically for Indian students: Internshala (large volume, mostly startups), LetsIntern, Unstop (formerly Dare2Compete), and LinkedIn are your primary channels. Internshala has many legitimate openings from funded startups and mid-size companies — don't dismiss it. The key is applying with a tailored cover letter on each platform, not mass-applying with a generic resume. Treating Internshala as a last resort because it's not 'prestigious' — Internshala has openings from Razorpay, Zepto, and many funded startups that also post there alongside AngelList. The platform doesn't determine the opportunity quality. Getting a technical interview through any channel — the company doesn't care where they found you once you're in the process.

Document everything you build during your internship while you're building it — don't try to reconstruct it 3 months later for your resume. Note the problem you solved, the technical approach you chose and why, the metrics before and after, and the scale it operates at. This documentation takes 15 minutes after completing each feature and pays dividends for years on your resume and in interviews. Finishing the internship and then vaguely writing 'worked on backend' in the resume because the details are foggy — specific details are only possible if you recorded them during the internship. Having 3-4 specific, metric-backed bullet points ready for your resume on the final day of the internship — this means you tracked your work well.

If you have zero experience and need to break in, consider building in public: tweet/post about what you're learning, share your projects in communities (Reddit r/learnprogramming, developer Discord servers, LinkedIn), and engage authentically in technical discussions. This creates a digital trail of evidence that you're actively developing. Several internship offers have come through Twitter DMs and Discord connections — it's unorthodox but real. Building projects in private and only sharing them when 'complete' — complete projects take much longer and you miss the community feedback that makes the project better anyway. Share early and iterate publicly. Getting a DM asking about your work, or a comment on your code from someone you don't know — that's when your public presence is working.

The PPO process differs by company: Amazon and Flipkart have structured PPO processes with a clear criteria, smaller companies make it ad hoc. At large companies, ask your recruiter (not manager) about the PPO criteria in week 1 — get it in writing if possible. This way you know exactly what you're measured on. Optimize for those criteria, not generic 'work hard' advice. Not knowing that your company even has a PPO process until week 8 of a 10-week internship — by then it's too late to course-correct. Ask about it in week 1. A formal mid-internship performance review with positive feedback is the strongest signal — if your company gives them, a 'exceeds expectations' in the mid-review nearly guarantees a PPO.

For 2nd year off-campus applications, your GitHub and LinkedIn do more work than your resume because you don't have much to put on a resume yet. Before applying anywhere, spend 2 weeks making sure your GitHub shows active development (at least 1 meaningful project, consistent commits), and your LinkedIn headline is targeted. Then apply to 30+ startups on AngelList, Internshala, and via cold email. The volume is your friend in 2nd year. Applying only to companies you recognize by name — the best 2nd year internship experiences often come from companies you've never heard of that become very impressive on your resume by the time you graduate. One technical interview by mid-January (for summer) — this means your profile is strong enough to clear initial filters. If zero interviews from 30 applications, resume/GitHub needs work.

For 2nd year internship interviews, the bar is lower than you think — startups primarily want to see: can you code in any language, do you understand basic CS concepts, and are you motivated to learn? Easy-medium LeetCode problems, the ability to explain your GitHub projects, and genuine enthusiasm for the company are sufficient. You don't need advanced DP or graph theory for most 2nd year startup internship interviews. Over-preparing for internship interviews as if they're full-time SWE interviews — spending 3 months on hard LeetCode before applying is a waste of time in 2nd year. Build projects and apply earlier. Clearing a technical interview where you could answer 80% of technical questions and explain your project clearly — you'll know the vibe when it goes well.

The most underused advantage in 2nd year is that companies have less competition for their 2nd year intern slots. The volume of 3rd year applicants is much higher than 2nd year because most students don't apply in 2nd year. Use this to your advantage: apply for 'internship open to 2nd year' roles, reach out to startups that haven't posted specific year requirements. Standing out in a less crowded pool is easier than standing out in a crowded 3rd year pool. Assuming 2nd year is too early to apply — many companies explicitly accept 2nd year interns and many more will consider you if you reach out directly. Waiting costs you a year of experience. Any technical internship experience by end of 2nd year summer is a success — the quality of company matters less than the fact of having real coding experience.

Evaluating a startup internship offer: ask how many engineers are on the team, what the tech stack is, who will be your mentor, and what a day looks like for the engineering team. Red flags: the internship is entirely unpaid with no clear project scope, the team is just 2 founders with no senior engineering mentor for you, or the 'project' is undefined. Green flags: they have a specific project in mind, they can name your potential mentor, they have a product in market. Accepting any startup internship without evaluating quality — 3 months at a startup where you do nothing meaningful is worse than 3 months building a project independently. Be selective even within startups. Being assigned a project with a clear deliverable in week 1 — this means the company has thought about intern onboarding and you'll produce something you can put on your resume.
